claim: Strait of Hormuz effectively closed to US/Israel-linked vessels

sub_questions.question: Who originally claimed that the Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to US/Israel-linked vessels?

findings.summary: The Islamic Revolution Guard Corps (IRGC) of Iran announced the prohibition of passage for vessels to and from ports of countries allied with and supporting US and Israel.
findings.quote: Iran’s Islamic Revolution Guard Corps (IRGC) announced on Friday that passage is prohibited for any vessel through any corridor in the Strait of Hormuz to and from the ports of countries allied with and supporting US and Israel.

sub_questions.question: What is the precise meaning of 'effectively closed' in this context (e.g., physical blockade, threat, advisory)?

findings.summary: It means prohibition of passage for US/Israel-linked vessels, enforced by the IRGC turning back ships, warnings, and declaring severe consequences. Iranian official later clarified it means ships require Iranian authorization and toll payment.
findings.quote: Besides, the IRGC Navy declared the Strait of Hormuz is closed, warning that any transit will face severe consequences according to the statement.

findings.summary: Ebrahim Azizi, head of the Iranian parliament's National Security Commission, said Saturday the strait was 'returning to the status quo.' He said earlier that this means ships would require Iranian authorization and toll payment before transiting.
findings.quote: Ebrahim Azizi, head of the Iranian parliament's National Security Commission, said Saturday the strait was 'returning to the status quo.' He said earlier that this means ships would require Iranian authorization and toll payment before transiting.

sub_questions.question: When was this claim first made and what was the initial context or source?

findings.summary: The claim was first made by the IRGC on March 27, 2026, in retaliation for US/Israel air war and assassination of supreme leader.
findings.quote: Iran’s Islamic Revolution Guard Corps (IRGC) announced on Friday that passage is prohibited for any vessel through any corridor in the Strait of Hormuz to and from the ports of countries allied with and supporting US and Israel.

sub_questions.question: Are there any official statements or reports from US, Israeli, or international maritime authorities confirming or denying restrictions on vessels in the Strait of Hormuz?

findings.summary: US President Donald Trump stated on March 2, 2026, that the US Navy would begin escorting oil tankers through the Strait. On April 12, 2026, Trump announced the US Navy would begin blocking ships entering/leaving the Strait, instructing them to interdict vessels paying tolls to Iran. CENTCOM announced a blockade of all maritime traffic entering/exiting Iranian ports starting April 13, 2026, clarifying it would not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting to/from non-Iranian ports.
findings.quote: March 2: US President Donald Trump stated that the US Navy would begin escorting oil tankers through the Strait "as soon as possible." [...] April 12: President Trump announced that the U.S. Navy would begin blocking ships entering or leaving the Strait of Hormuz. He instructed the Navy to "seek and interdict every vessel in International Waters that has paid a toll to Iran." [...] April 12: U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM) announced that US forces would begin implementing a blockade of "all maritime traffic entering and exiting Iranian ports" starting April 13 at 10 a.m. Eastern Time. CENTCOM clarified that this blockade would be enforced "impartially" against vessels of all nations entering or departing Iranian ports, but "will not impede freedom of navigation for vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz to and from non-Iranian ports."

findings.summary: The International Maritime Organization (IMO) expressed deep concern, stating there is 'no safe transit anywhere in the Strait of Hormuz,' and stressed that freedom of navigation is a fundamental principle of international maritime law, rejecting any imposition of tolls or discriminatory transit measures. They confirmed 29 attacks on vessels and warned of mines.
findings.quote: IMO Secretary-General Arsenio Dominguez has repeatedly voiced deep concern over attacks on merchant vessels... Dominguez stated unequivocally that "there is no safe transit anywhere in the Strait of Hormuz," urging maximum caution from all shipping companies. He advised vessels to avoid transiting the affected region until conditions improve. The IMO stressed that freedom of navigation is a fundamental principle of international maritime law and must be respected by all parties. They reject any imposition of tolls, fees, or discriminatory transit measures for passage through international straits.
